title: Emergency Funds: Money for When Life Happens
summary: Not a savings goal — a shock absorber for the unexpected.
doThisToday: If you don't already have money set aside for surprises, open a separate savings spot today and drop in whatever you can — even $10 counts as a start.

cards.prompt: An emergency fund is money set aside for one job only: covering unexpected costs.
cards.explanation: Not concert tickets. Not a sale you don't want to miss. A cracked phone screen, a flat tire, a surprise fee — the stuff that happens whether you planned for it or not.

cards.prompt: Sort each situation into Real Emergency or Not an Emergency.

items.label: Video game is 50% off this weekend

cards.explanation: Real emergencies are unplanned AND necessary. Sales and drops are just wants with a deadline attached to make them feel urgent — that's marketing, not an emergency.

cards.prompt: True or false: your emergency fund should be easy to access quickly, not locked away somewhere hard to reach.
cards.explanation: Emergencies don't wait. A regular savings account you can transfer from in a day or two is ideal — not something tied up for months, which defeats the purpose.

cards.prompt: Adults are often told to save 3-6 months of essential expenses. As a teen, that math doesn't really apply yet.
cards.explanation: Your version: aim for enough to cover the specific surprises that actually hit teens — a phone repair, a contribution toward a car repair, replacing something that breaks. Often $100-300 covers most of it.

cards.prompt: Your phone screen cracks and repair costs $150. You have $200 in an emergency fund. What happens?
options: You panic-borrow from a friend or put it on a parent's card with stress
You pay for the repair from the fund, no panic, no debt, no derailing your other savings goals
You just live with a cracked screen forever
cards.explanation: That's the entire point of the fund — it absorbs the shock so a bad-luck moment doesn't wreck your budget or force you into debt.

cards.prompt: You have both a car-fund goal AND no emergency fund yet. What's usually the smarter order?
options: Build at least a small emergency fund first, so a surprise expense doesn't force you to raid your car savings
Ignore the emergency fund completely and focus only on the car
It doesn't matter which comes first
cards.explanation: Without an emergency cushion, any surprise cost eats into your other goals — so a small emergency fund usually comes first, even before bigger dream-goal savings.

cards.prompt: You don't need $1,000 saved to call it an emergency fund. You need SOME amount set aside for exactly this purpose, and nothing else.
cards.explanation: Start with whatever you can. The habit of having a separate "only for real emergencies" fund matters more than the size, at least at the start.
